Vol. I · 30 May 2026
A Swiss Alps Diary

The Kindness of a Stranger

What I learned when an electric car, a Google Map, and a wooden chalet all conspired against me — and a kind man in the Valais saved our trip.

By A Traveller, Age 70  ·  Val d'Hérens, Switzerland

A morning view from the village — the kind of postcard that lures you here in the first place.

There is a moment, just before things go wrong, when a traveller feels invincible. We had booked the chalet. We had the rental car charged. We had the booking confirmation on the phone, with a Google Map showing exactly where to go. We were, in that confident half-hour, the most prepared travellers in Switzerland.

What I did not yet know was that in the next few hours I would be standing helpless beside a fast-charger I could not operate, in a meadow I could not name, in front of a wooden door I could not unlock. I would also meet a man in a red shirt who would, very quietly, change the whole shape of the trip.

This is the story of that day — and a few honest warnings for anyone planning to do what we did.

Chapter OneThe Booking That Lied (a little)

When you book a place called Cocon on a travel app and the screen shows you a tidy little map with the name floating in a blue speech bubble, your brain fills in the rest. A reception desk. A friendly host. A driveway. A sign.

This is the picture you are sold:

The booking app, the night before. A red circle drawn on a map, a name in a bubble. It all looks so simple from the sofa.

What the app does not show you is that in a Swiss alpine village, houses are numbered by some ancient logic that locals understand and no one else does. There is no reception. There is no host waiting. The "address" on Chemin de L'Emounate is a wooden chalet two hundred years old, sitting in a meadow of wildflowers, looking exactly like the seven other wooden chalets sitting in meadows of wildflowers around it.

And the key? The key is in a small black combination box bolted to a wooden post.

This is your "reception." Four little rotating dials and a code your host emailed you three days ago. Hope you saved it offline.

Chapter TwoThe Electric Car Problem

We were driving a rented electric car — a white SUV, brand new, beautifully silent. In theory, charming. In practice, the cause of two hours of mild panic.

If you have never tried to charge an EV in rural Switzerland, allow me to describe the experience. You arrive at a charging station in a beautiful spot — usually beside a forest, or in a parking lot beside a pass road. There is a sleek machine that says 66 kW · DC Fast Charger. You plug the cable in. Nothing happens. You open the app. The app wants a login. You make a login. The login wants a credit card. You give it a credit card. The app freezes. You unplug. You replug. You stand there in the alpine sun, an old man defeated by a Swiss machine.

The sun was getting lower. The battery was getting lower. And we were one hour from anywhere with a person.

I did what any reasonable retiree would do. I walked to the edge of the road and I waved my arms at the next passing car.

Chapter ThreeThe Man in the Red Shirt

He stopped. He didn't have to, but he did. A tall, smiling Swiss man in a bright red polo shirt, who took one look at our predicament and said, in careful English, "Ah — yes. Let me show
